title: A Short History of Boulder Creek, California
excerpt: Boulder Creek's story runs in three chapters: a scatter of sawmill settlements in the 1860s and 1870s, a rail-served lumber boomtown from 1885 to about 1915, and the quieter mountain community and Big Basin gateway it has been ever since.
bodyHtml:
Boulder Creek's story runs in three chapters: a scatter of sawmill settlements in the 1860s and 1870s, a rail-served lumber boomtown from 1885 to about 1915, and the quieter mountain community and Big Basin gateway it has been ever since. The redwoods explain all of it. The trees brought the mills, the mills brought a flume and then a railroad, and once the logging wound down, the forest that survived (much of it protected in Big Basin) became the reason people kept coming up Highway 9. Here's how each chapter unfolded, with sources you can check yourself.

The valley's first people

Long before anyone milled a board here, this was a lived-in landscape. People have been in the Santa Cruz region for more than 10,000 years, according to the Santa Cruz Museum of Natural History, and the Indigenous people of this area spoke Awaswas, one of at least eight languages in the Ohlone language family.

Their descendants are still here. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band is made up of the surviving descendant families of the Indigenous people taken into Mission Santa Cruz (Awaswas speakers) and Mission San Juan Bautista (Mutsun speakers). Today the tribe works through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous knowledge and stewardship across ancestral lands that run from Ano Nuevo along the Santa Cruz Mountains to Monterey Bay. That work is happening now, in these mountains, not just in the past tense.

Sawmills, a flume, and a town called Lorenzo

The town's founding wasn't a single event. Rail historian Derek Whaley, whose Santa Cruz Trains project documents the county's railroad era in detail, traces the settlement to a small sawmill Joseph Wilbur Peery put up in the early 1860s. By 1872 a post office named Boulder Creek was operating out of the general store, and the name stuck.

The first big change came in 1875, when the San Lorenzo Valley Flume & Lumber Company completed a V-shaped flume that carried rough lumber down the valley to Felton. The flume turned the little settlement into a company town, and in 1879 the South Pacific Coast Railroad bought the flume company outright.

Boulder Creek also wasn't the only town on this stretch of the river. A separate village called Lorenzo grew up along the county road between today's Boulder Creek and Brookdale. The San Lorenzo Valley Historical Society notes that its museum building, the original Grace Episcopal Church, stood "in what was originally the town of Lorenzo." Lorenzo didn't last, but its church did, and you can visit it today.

The railroad years, 1885 to 1915

Flumes leak, jam, and lose lumber, so the railroad came next. Crews extended a line up the valley from Felton, and the first train rolled into Boulder Creek in April 1885. The town became the end of the line: the shipping point for the mills working the upper San Lorenzo Valley and the ridges toward Big Basin.

The boom that followed was real. Whaley writes that Boulder Creek was "one of the largest exporters of lumber in the United States from 1885 to 1915," and the year-round population passed 800 at the height of it. The San Lorenzo Valley Chamber of Commerce describes the whole valley as a logging center in those years, especially while San Francisco rebuilt after the 1906 earthquake. Local business owners organized a San Lorenzo Valley Board of Trade around the turn of the century; the chamber that succeeded it formed in 1926 and is still going.

The end of the line

It couldn't last, and it didn't. By the early twentieth century the big timber close to the rail line was mostly gone and logging began to decline. The Dougherty Extension line above town was abandoned around 1917. In March 1931 Southern Pacific replaced the passenger trains with Pacific Greyhound bus service, and in January 1934 the last freight train left Boulder Creek.

Whaley's summary of what came next is hard to improve on: the town "transitioned begrudgingly from a lumber town to a tourist town." The sawmill economy gave way to summer cabins, resorts, and day visitors headed for the big trees.

Big Basin changes the story

The reason those big trees were still standing is its own piece of Boulder Creek history. In May 1900, while mills were still running full tilt, a group of conservationists led by San Jose photographer Andrew P. Hill camped in Big Basin and formed the Sempervirens Club to save the old growth from logging. The state historical landmark at the site, registered as Landmark 827, dates the founding camp at the base of Slippery Rock to May 15, 1900. Two years of lobbying later, the state bought thousands of acres of ancient forest, and the Sempervirens Fund (the club's modern successor) notes that the 1902 purchase created "the first park established in California in the current state park system."

That decision reshaped Boulder Creek's future. Once the logs stopped moving, the park kept the town on the map. It still does: Highway 236 to Big Basin leaves Highway 9 right in downtown Boulder Creek, and the park entrance kiosk is nine miles up the road.

Fire and recovery

The hardest recent chapter came in August 2020, when the CZU Lightning Complex fire burned through the mountains. The Sempervirens Fund calls it "the most devastating fire ever recorded in the Santa Cruz mountains region," with more than 86,000 acres burned. California State Parks reports the fire swept through 97 percent of Big Basin and destroyed the park's historic buildings, though most of the old-growth redwoods survived.

The fire also destroyed homes throughout the valley's communities, and rebuilding has taken years. Santa Cruz County's own 2024 Grand Jury report on the fire's aftermath is titled "Four Years Later The Flame Still Burns," which tells you how long recovery runs in mountain country. Anyone who lived here through it doesn't need the reminder.

Boulder Creek today

Boulder Creek never incorporated as a city; it remains an unincorporated community in Santa Cruz County, and the chamber counts more than 35,000 people across the San Lorenzo Valley as a whole. The lumber wharves and rail yards are long gone, but the town still works the way the railroad laid it out, with the commercial strip along Highway 9 and the road to Big Basin splitting off at the center of it.

If you want to go deeper than this article, start with the San Lorenzo Valley Museum. The historical society behind it was founded in Boulder Creek in March 1976, opened the museum in the old Grace Episcopal Church in December 1999, and now runs two galleries: the Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek and the Faye G. Belardi Memorial Gallery in Felton. Both buildings are former churches listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Gallery hours and exhibits change, so check the museum's site before you make the trip.

Frequently Asked Questions

When was Boulder Creek founded?

There's no single founding date. The settlement began with a sawmill in the early 1860s, got its post office (and its name) in 1872, and took its lasting shape when the railroad arrived in April 1885. Because the town never incorporated, there's no incorporation date either.

Why is it called Boulder Creek?

The town took its name from the Boulder Creek post office, established in 1872 and named for the creek that joins the San Lorenzo River near downtown. Neighboring settlements called Boulder and Lorenzo faded, and the post office name is the one that survived.

How big was Boulder Creek during the lumber boom?

The year-round population passed 800 in the boom years, according to rail historian Derek Whaley, who also ranks the town among the largest lumber exporters in the country between 1885 and 1915. The seasonal workforce in the surrounding mill camps came and went with the cutting.

Where can I see Boulder Creek history in person?

The San Lorenzo Valley Museum's Grace Episcopal Gallery at 12547 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek is the closest stop, with a second gallery in Felton. Hours change seasonally, so confirm on slvmuseum.org before visiting. The Slippery Rock landmark commemorating the Sempervirens Club is inside Big Basin, where access depends on current park conditions.

title: How Big Basin Became California's First State Park
excerpt: Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, after a two-year campaign by the Sempervirens Club, a citizens' group formed in the basin itself in May 1900 to stop the last big stands of old-growth redwood from being logged.
bodyHtml:
Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, after a two-year campaign by the Sempervirens Club, a citizens' group formed in the basin itself in May 1900 to stop the last big stands of old-growth redwood from being logged. The purchase created California Redwood Park, which the Sempervirens Fund describes as "the first park established in California in the current state park system." The whole thing started nine miles up the road from Boulder Creek, and it began with a photographer who refused to hand over his negatives.

The basin before the park

People were part of this forest long before the preservation fight. California State Parks notes that humans lived in or near Big Basin for at least 10,000 years, and it identifies the Cotoni and Quiroste peoples, part of the broader Ohlone culture of the San Francisco and Monterey Bay areas, as the tribes of this landscape. They harvested seeds and acorns, wove baskets, and hunted elk and deer in these canyons.

That history isn't past tense. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, made up of the surviving descendant families of Indigenous people taken into Mission Santa Cruz (Awaswas speakers) and Mission San Juan Bautista (Mutsun speakers), works today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust to restore Indigenous stewardship across ancestral lands that run from Ano Nuevo through these mountains to Monterey Bay. State Parks adds that descendants of the basin's tribes are working toward federal recognition.

By the late 1800s, the same forest was being cut fast. Sawmills worked the San Lorenzo Valley below, Boulder Creek was shipping lumber by rail, and the old growth in the basin was privately owned timberland with a short life expectancy.

A photographer refuses to hand over his negatives

The usual telling starts with Andrew P. Hill, a San Jose photographer. According to State Parks' cultural history, Hill photographed redwoods at Welch's Grove in the Santa Cruz Mountains, and the grove's owner accused him of trespassing and demanded his negatives. Hill refused, and vowed to himself to save the trees for future generations. The run-in came in 1899 or 1900, depending on the account; the Sempervirens Fund dates the start of his campaign to 1899.

Either way, Hill went home to San Jose and started organizing. A meeting at Stanford University in May 1900 pulled together journalists, politicians, artists, business owners, and scholars, and the group headed into the mountains to see the threatened forest for themselves.

Thirty-two dollars and a two-year campaign

What happened next is commemorated by a bronze plaque inside the park. California Historical Landmark 827 records that "a group of conservationists led by Andrew P. Hill camped at the base of Slippery Rock on May 15, 1900 and formed the Sempervirens Club to preserve the redwoods of Big Basin." The Sempervirens Fund's own history puts the founding gathering on May 18, along Sempervirens Creek, so accounts differ by a few days. On the substance they agree: a small group camped in the basin, decided the forest had to be saved, passed a hat, and collected 32 dollars to finance the effort.

Club member Carrie Stevens Walter put the goal plainly in 1901, writing of preserving "this magnificent domain of redwood forests," a line State Parks still quotes on the park's page. The club spent more than two years lobbying Sacramento. In 1901 the legislature passed the enabling legislation (unanimously, by State Parks' account), and in 1902 the State of California bought the heart of the basin: roughly 3,500 to 3,800 acres of ancient forest, depending on the account. The landmark plaque dates the deeding of the land to September 20, 1902, and calls it the beginning of the California State Park System.

From California Redwood Park to Big Basin Redwoods

The new park was called California Redwood Park, and it grew from there. State Parks records that the park was renamed Big Basin Redwoods in 1927, and additional purchases (including 3,901 acres acquired from the Big Basin Lumber Company in 1906) kept expanding it. Today the park preserves more than 18,000 acres, including the largest continuous stand of ancient coast redwoods south of San Francisco, with trees between 1,000 and 1,800 years old.

The 1930s gave the park the look longtime visitors remember. Civilian Conservation Corps crews built the redwood Nature Lodge and Park Headquarters, a campfire center, a footbridge, cabins, and much of the trail network.

The club that started it all kept going too. After helping establish Castle Rock State Park in the late 1960s, the organization renamed itself the Sempervirens Fund in 1968, and it's still buying and protecting redwood forest in these mountains today.

The fire that changed everything

On August 18, 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex fire swept through 97 percent of the park's property, according to State Parks. Every historic structure was lost, including the CCC-era headquarters and lodge. The Sempervirens Fund calls the CZU fire, which burned more than 86,000 acres across the Santa Cruz Mountains, the most devastating fire ever recorded in the region.

The trees told a different story than the buildings. State Parks reports that most of the old-growth redwoods survived, and new growth has been coming back across the burned landscape ever since. Coast redwoods have lived through fire for a very long time; the visitor infrastructure hadn't.

What Big Basin looks like now

The park partially reopened in July 2022 and has been recovering in stages since. As of mid-2026, the park's official page lists day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, strongly recommends parking reservations because spaces are limited, and notes that all campgrounds remain closed. Individual trails open and close as work continues, so check current conditions on parks.ca.gov before you drive up; this article can't promise what will be open on any given day. The Rancho del Oso side of the park, off Highway 1 north of Santa Cruz, is open with no entrance fee.

The long-term rebuild runs through Reimagining Big Basin, State Parks' multi-year planning effort. A public visioning process wrapped up in summer 2022, and the project has since produced a Facilities Management Plan, a General Plan Amendment, and an environmental impact report, with a State Park and Recreation Commission hearing on the plans held July 2, 2026. The stated goal is to reestablish facilities that support visitors while protecting the forest that made the park worth fighting for in the first place.

Boulder Creek, the gateway then and now

Boulder Creek's own history is tangled up in this story. The town shipped the lumber that came out of these mountains, and when the logging economy faded in the early twentieth century, it leaned into visitors headed for the park instead. Rail historian Derek Whaley's Santa Cruz Trains research describes Boulder Creek transitioning "from a lumber town to a tourist town" built around attractions like Big Basin.

The geography hasn't changed. The park's official directions start here: from Highway 9 in downtown Boulder Creek, turn north onto Highway 236, and the entrance kiosk is nine miles up the road. If you're headed to the park, town is the last stop for gas, food, and supplies, the same role it's played for well over a century.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Big Basin open right now?

Partially. As of mid-2026 the park is open for day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, parking reservations are strongly recommended, and all campgrounds are closed while rebuilding continues. Trail openings change as recovery work progresses, so check parks.ca.gov before you go.

Did the old-growth redwoods survive the 2020 fire?

Most did, according to State Parks. The CZU fire burned through 97 percent of the park and destroyed every historic building, but coast redwoods are adapted to fire, and the surviving giants have been putting out new growth since. The forest looks different than it did before 2020, and it's still recovering.

What does "California's first state park" actually mean?

Big Basin, established in 1902 as California Redwood Park, was the first park in what became the current California state park system. The state historical landmark at Slippery Rock marks the 1902 land transfer as the beginning of the California State Park System, and State Parks calls Big Basin its oldest park.

How do I get to Big Basin from Boulder Creek?

Take Highway 236 north from Highway 9 in downtown Boulder Creek; the park entrance kiosk is nine miles up. The road is narrow and winding, so allow more time than the mileage suggests, and confirm current access before you go.

Who lived in Big Basin before it was a park?

State Parks identifies the Cotoni and Quiroste peoples, part of the Ohlone culture, and notes that people lived in or near the basin for at least 10,000 years. The Amah Mutsun Tribal Band, descended from Indigenous people taken into the Santa Cruz and San Juan Bautista missions, stewards ancestral lands in these mountains today through the Amah Mutsun Land Trust.

title: Fires, Floods, and Storms: The Events That Shaped Boulder Creek
excerpt: If you've ever wondered why Boulder Creek neighbors talk about go-bags, generator plans, and river levels the way other towns talk about traffic, the answer is in the record.
bodyHtml:
If you've ever wondered why Boulder Creek neighbors talk about go-bags, generator plans, and river levels the way other towns talk about traffic, the answer is in the record. The San Lorenzo Valley has lived through a documented series of hard events: damaging floods in 1940, 1955, and 1958, the deadly storm and landslides of January 1982, the CZU Lightning Complex fire in 2020, and the atmospheric river storms of January 2023. This article walks through what actually happened in each one, using flood reports, USGS studies, and CAL FIRE records, and points you to the official alert systems that exist because of this history.

Why this valley sees big water and big fire

Boulder Creek sits near the top of the San Lorenzo River watershed, in a steep, forested canyon that catches enormous amounts of rain. The numbers back that up. During the December 1955 storm, the Boulder Creek Locatelli Ranch station recorded 9.11 inches of rain in a single 24-hour period, and 20.11 inches over about two weeks, according to a US Army Corps of Engineers flood report digitized by Santa Cruz Public Libraries. In January 1982, the US Geological Survey documented a storm that dropped as much as half the region's average annual precipitation in about 32 hours.

Steep slopes, saturated soil, a fast river, and a forest that evolved with fire. That combination has shaped the valley's biggest days, and it's why the events below still come up in conversation here.

The Christmas flood of December 1955

The flood most old-timers still measure everything against arrived on the evening of December 22, 1955. A Santa Cruz Public Libraries factsheet calls it the highest flooding in Santa Cruz history. On the upper river near Boulder Creek, the water ran roughly 3 to 3.5 feet higher than the previous flood of record from 1940, according to the Corps report.

The damage ran the length of the valley. Heavy rain scoured out large trees, which floated downstream and jammed at narrow points in the channel, backing up water and shoving the river out of its normal alignment. Bridges, road fills, and small channel dams were undercut and washed out. A Santa Cruz Sentinel-News account from December 25, 1955, quoted in the Corps report, put it plainly: nearly 100 homes along the river were destroyed, and Highway 9 was blocked by slides and downed trees. The road to Big Basin was closed too.

Seven people lost their lives in the San Lorenzo River Basin during the flood, five in the city of Santa Cruz and two near Mount Hermon, and an estimated 2,830 people were displaced. In Boulder Creek itself, the recorded damage was lighter than downstream, mostly scoured channel banks, damaged cabin camps, and undercut bridge approaches. The lesson the Corps drew is worth remembering: many people had heard the warnings on local radio but didn't believe the water could rise that far.

A smaller flood followed in April 1958. It was minor by comparison, but it still forced about 250 people in the northern part of the county from their homes and caused an estimated $170,000 in damage.

January 1982: the storm and the Love Creek slide

The storm of January 3 through 5, 1982, was a different kind of event. Rain fell so hard and so fast that hillsides across the region gave way. The USGS documented more than 18,000 slides that turned into debris flows across the San Francisco Bay region, and landslides accounted for 25 of the storm's 33 deaths.

The worst of it happened just down the valley from Boulder Creek. Above the community of Love Creek, near Ben Lomond, more than 459,000 cubic meters of earth and rock slid off a mountainside and buried ten people in their homes. Countywide, the Santa Cruz Public Libraries factsheet records 22 dead, 50 injured, 135 homes destroyed, 300 damaged, and more than $106 million in combined public and private losses. Unlike 1955, when most of the damage hit the city of Santa Cruz, about 90 percent of the 1982 damage fell outside city limits, in the mountains and valley.

That storm is the reason debris flow risk is treated as seriously as river flooding here, especially on slopes that have burned.

August 2020: the CZU Lightning Complex fire

The CZU Lightning Complex began with lightning strikes on August 16, 2020, and became the event that most directly shaped the Boulder Creek of today. According to CAL FIRE's incident record, the fire burned 86,509 acres across Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties and was declared contained on September 22, 2020, after 37 days. It destroyed 1,490 structures, damaged 140 more, and took one civilian life.

Within Santa Cruz County, the losses centered on the San Lorenzo Valley and the ridges around it. Lookout Santa Cruz has reported the county's tally of 911 homes destroyed, a number this community knows without needing to look it up. Boulder Creek's own fire department served in the unified command that fought the fire, alongside CAL FIRE and neighboring districts.

The fire also burned 97 percent of Big Basin Redwoods State Park, the park this town has been the gateway to since 1902. Most of the old-growth redwoods survived, and the park has been reopening in stages since July 2022.

Years later, rebuilding is still under way, and many neighbors are still working through permits, insurance, and construction. That's part of daily life here, not a closed chapter, and it's the main reason this site treats preparedness as a permanent topic rather than a seasonal one.

January 2023: atmospheric rivers on a burn scar

The winter of 2023 tested the valley again. A series of atmospheric river storms delivered unusually large amounts of rain in January 2023, enough that the USGS deployed temporary rain gauges in the county, including one in Boulder Creek along Hilton Drive, specifically to support emergency response and track how the landscape was holding up after the fire. Roads and trails in and around Big Basin that had been reopening after the fire took new damage, and Sempervirens Fund notes that park access has continued to evolve because of both the fire and those storms.

The 2023 storms made the modern pattern clear: in this valley, fire and flood aren't separate stories. A burned slope sheds water differently for years, so a wet winter after a fire year deserves extra respect.

What this history means for living here now

The practical takeaway isn't fear. It's the habits this town already has: know your evacuation routes, keep the truck fueled in storm season, and sign up for official alerts before you need them.

Santa Cruz County's alert system is CruzAware, and you can register through the county's alert notification page to get warnings by text, phone call, email, or the CruzAware app. For weather itself, the National Weather Service San Francisco Bay Area/Monterey office issues the forecasts, watches, and warnings that cover Santa Cruz County. In any immediate emergency, call 911. This site shares history and pointers, but it never interprets live emergency conditions, and neither should any website. Official alerts and dispatchers do that job.

If the history itself pulls you in, the San Lorenzo Valley Museum keeps a gallery right in Boulder Creek at 12547 Highway 9, and it's the natural next stop for photographs and records of the valley's floods, fires, and the people who rebuilt after each one.

Frequently Asked Questions

What was the worst flood in Boulder Creek history?

The December 1955 flood is the flood of record for the San Lorenzo River. Per the Army Corps of Engineers report, it exceeded the previous record flood of 1940 by roughly 3 to 3.5 feet near Boulder Creek, killed seven people in the river basin, and displaced an estimated 2,830 residents.

How big was the CZU fire, and how did it affect Boulder Creek?

Per CAL FIRE, the CZU Lightning Complex started August 16, 2020, burned 86,509 acres in Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties, destroyed 1,490 structures, and caused one civilian death. The losses in Santa Cruz County were concentrated in and around the San Lorenzo Valley, including hundreds of homes in the Boulder Creek area.

How do I sign up for local emergency alerts?

Register for CruzAware through the county's alert notification page. You can receive alerts by text, call, email, or the mobile app. Pair that with forecasts from the National Weather Service Monterey office, and call 911 in any immediate emergency.

Why does a fire make future floods worse?

Burned slopes absorb less water and shed soil and debris more easily, which raises the risk of flooding and debris flows for years afterward. That's why the USGS put rain gauges in Boulder Creek during the January 2023 storms, and why post-fire winters get extra attention here.

Did Big Basin survive the fire?

The fire burned 97 percent of the park and nearly all of its buildings, but most of the old-growth redwoods survived and the park has been reopening in stages since July 2022. Check the official state parks page before visiting, since access is still limited and changes.

title: "Boulder Creek Emergency Contacts: The One-Page List"
excerpt: For any emergency in Boulder Creek, call 911. That's the whole first rule, and it covers fire, medical, crime in progress, a downed power line, or any situation where someone could get hurt.
bodyHtml:
For any emergency in Boulder Creek, call 911. That's the whole first rule, and it covers fire, medical, crime in progress, a downed power line, or any situation where someone could get hurt. Everything below is for the moments that aren't 911 calls: the tree across a county road, the water main bubbling up on your street, the outage that's stretched into hour six. Every number and link on this page was checked against the agency's own website on July 15, 2026, and we re-verify the list quarterly. Print it, stick it on the fridge, and you'll never have to dig through a dozen sites during a storm again.

When to Call 911

Call 911 for anything that threatens life or property right now: smoke or flames, a medical emergency, a crash with injuries, a crime happening in front of you, or a power line on the ground. Don't worry about whether it's "bad enough." Dispatchers would rather sort that out than have you wait. Everything else on this page is for situations that are a problem but not an emergency.

Fire: Boulder Creek Fire Protection District

The Boulder Creek Fire Protection District is our local fire agency, based at 13230 Central Ave in the middle of town.

The district office line is (831) 338-7222. Use it for district business: questions about the department, defensible space, permits, and community programs. It is not a dispatch line. If something is burning, call 911.

For wildfire information at the regional level, CAL FIRE covers our area through its San Mateo-Santa Cruz Unit (CZU), headquartered at 6059 Highway 9 in Felton. The unit office number is (831) 335-5353, again for administrative questions rather than reporting fires.

Law Enforcement: Santa Cruz County Sheriff

Boulder Creek is unincorporated, so law enforcement is the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office.

For a deputy when it's not an emergency (a cold burglary, ongoing noise, suspicious activity that's already over), call non-emergency dispatch at (831) 471-1121. The main Sheriff's Office line is (831) 454-7600 for records and general business. The full list of division numbers is on the Sheriff's phone directory.

There's also a Boulder Creek Sheriff's Service Center right in town at 13210 Central Avenue, reachable at (831) 454-7440. It's a satellite office, not a dispatch center, so hours vary. Call before you drive over.

Roads: County Public Works and Caltrans

Road problems split two ways in Boulder Creek, and knowing which agency owns the road saves you a transfer.

County-maintained roads (most of the neighborhood roads off the highway) belong to Santa Cruz County Public Works, part of the county's Community Development and Infrastructure department. Their road dispatch line, (831) 477-3999, is answered 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and it's the number for a tree down across a county road, a slide, a flooded section, or a failing culvert. The department's main line is (831) 454-2160, weekdays 8 to noon and 1 to 5. Current closures are posted on the county's Road Closure Dashboard, linked from the county's Transportation and Roads page.

Highway 9 and Highway 236 are state highways, so they're Caltrans territory. For current highway conditions, check the Caltrans road information page or call 1-800-427-7623 and enter the route number.

If a downed tree involves power lines, treat it as an emergency: call 911 and stay well away.

Power and Gas: PG&E

For outages, start at the PG&E outage center, where you can report an outage and check the outage map. PG&E's customer service line is 1-877-660-6789.

For a downed power line, PG&E's own guidance is blunt and worth repeating: call 911 first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000. Keep people and pets far away, and assume every line is live.

If you smell gas, leave the area first, then call 911. Report it to PG&E once you're somewhere safe; their outage and safety support page covers reporting options.

PG&E also offers outage updates by text, email, or phone if you sign up through your PG&E online account. Worth doing before storm season, since those alerts are tied to your service address.

Water: San Lorenzo Valley Water District

Most Boulder Creek homes get water from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, headquartered at 13060 Highway 9 in Boulder Creek.

One number covers everything: (831) 338-2153. During office hours (weekdays 8:30 to noon and 1 to 5, phones until 4:30) you'll reach staff directly. For a water emergency after hours, a main break, or a big leak, call the same number; the district says calls forward to an answering service that reaches their on-call staff. Details are on their customer service page.

If you're on a different system (some streets are served by small mutual water companies), your bill has the right number. Add it to your copy of this list.

Alerts: Sign Up for CruzAware

CruzAware is the county's emergency and non-emergency alert system, operated by the County of Santa Cruz with Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1. It sends evacuation notices, severe weather warnings, and other public safety alerts by text, phone call, and email. Signing up takes a few minutes at cruzaware.org, and the county's alert information page explains the system. No alert system can promise delivery in every situation, so registering more than one contact method is smart. We have a full walkthrough in our CruzAware setup guide.

Keep This List Where You Can Find It

Phones die in outages and cell service up here gets thin in a storm. A paper copy in a kitchen drawer costs you nothing. We re-check every number on this page against the agencies' own sites each quarter; if you ever find one that's changed before we do, tell us and we'll fix it.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who do I call about a tree down across my road?

If lines are involved or it's blocking an evacuation route during an active emergency, call 911. Otherwise: county road dispatch at (831) 477-3999 for county-maintained roads, any hour. If it's on Highway 9 or 236, it's Caltrans; check conditions or call 1-800-427-7623. On a private road, clearing it is generally the road association's job.

What's the non-emergency number for the Sheriff in Boulder Creek?

Call (831) 471-1121 for non-emergency dispatch when you need a deputy. The Boulder Creek Sheriff's Service Center on Central Avenue is (831) 454-7440 for local, non-urgent matters.

Who do I call when the power goes out?

Report it and track it through the PG&E outage center, or call 1-877-660-6789. If you see a downed line, that's a 911 call first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000.

Is there one number for water problems?

For San Lorenzo Valley Water District customers, yes: (831) 338-2153, day or night. After hours it forwards to an answering service that contacts on-call crews.

How current is this list?

Every contact was verified against the agency's own website on July 15, 2026, and we review the page quarterly. If an agency's information isn't listed here, go straight to that agency's site rather than guessing.

title: Power Outage Plan for Mountain Households
excerpt: A solid outage plan for a Boulder Creek household comes down to five things you set up before the wind picks up: alerts from PG&E and the county, a safe way to run backup power, stored water if you're on a well, a plan for food and medicine, and a habit of checking on the neighbors.
bodyHtml:
A solid outage plan for a Boulder Creek household comes down to five things you set up before the wind picks up: alerts from PG&E and the county, a safe way to run backup power, stored water if you're on a well, a plan for food and medicine, and a habit of checking on the neighbors. None of it is complicated, and most of it takes one weekend afternoon. If you only do one thing today, sign up for outage alerts and save PG&E's 24-hour outage line, 1-800-743-5002, in your phone. When the lights go out, PG&E's outage map and alerts are the source of truth for what's happening and when power might come back, and even their estimates can change as crews find the actual damage.

Anyone who's spent a winter up here knows outages come with the territory. Trees and power lines share the same narrow canyons, and the same wind that drops a fir across Highway 9 can take a line down with it. A little planning turns a three-day outage from a crisis into an inconvenience.

Know the Two Kinds of Outages

Most of our outages are storm outages: wind, falling trees and limbs, or equipment trouble. They arrive with little warning, and restoration in steep, wooded terrain can take longer than it does in town because crews have to find the damage before they can fix it.

The second kind is a Public Safety Power Shutoff, or PSPS. That's a planned shutoff PG&E calls during dry, windy, high fire-risk weather so its lines can't spark a wildfire. PG&E says it does its best to alert customers ahead of time by email, phone call, and text, but the company also notes that in some cases the first alert may not come until the same day the power goes off. The California Public Utilities Commission oversees these events and describes them as a measure of last resort. Either way, the practical lesson is the same: don't count on long notice. Have your plan ready before fire season, not during it.

Set Up Alerts Before You Need Them

Start with PG&E. If the account is in your name, you automatically get outage notifications for your service address, and you can fine-tune how you're contacted through your online account. If you rent, or you want updates on a parent's place or a second property, use PG&E's address-level alerts: search any address on the outage map, select "Get outage updates," and enter your phone or email. You don't need an online account for that, and PSPS address alerts are available by call and text too.

Then add the county layer. CruzAware is Santa Cruz County's alert and warning system, and it's how you'll hear about evacuations, road closures, and other emergencies that often travel with big outages. Sign up at cruzaware.org and pick how you want to be reached. The county also lists a quick option to text your ZIP code to 888777 for basic text alerts.

Finally, report what you see. If your power's been out a while and nothing shows on the map, report it through the PG&E Outage Center or call 1-800-743-5002. Outages in the far reaches of the district sometimes need a phone call before anyone knows a tap line is dead.

Generator Safety Comes First

A generator is the difference between a rough week and a manageable one, but it's also the most dangerous thing in this plan, so let's be direct about it.

Never run a generator inside your home or garage, even with the doors and windows open. The CDC says to run generators only outside, more than 20 feet from any windows, doors, and vents. Carbon monoxide is odorless and colorless, it kills more than 400 Americans a year, and people who are asleep can die before they ever feel sick. Put a battery-powered or battery-backup carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area, and check it when you check your smoke alarms. Ready.gov gives the same 20-foot rule and adds a few worth keeping: keep the generator dry and out of the rain, let it cool before refueling, and use heavy-duty extension cords rated for the load.

The second rule is about your wiring. Never plug a generator into a wall outlet or connect it directly to your panel without proper equipment. PG&E's backup power safety page is blunt about this: power that backfeeds from your generator into the lines can injure the crews working to restore your neighborhood. If you want the generator to run household circuits rather than extension cords, have an electrician install a transfer switch or equivalent equipment that isolates your home from the grid. And store fuel outside the house, never indoors.

If you're considering a battery system instead, the same isolation rules apply, and a licensed installer will handle them. Batteries sidestep the carbon monoxide problem entirely.

Water When the Well Pump Is Down

If you're on a private well, no electricity means no pump, which means no water. Plan for stored water as a matter of course: a common rule of thumb is one gallon per person per day, and more is better when outages can run multiple days. Cases of bottled water work, and so do food-grade containers you fill and rotate a couple of times a year. Keep separate buckets or jugs for flushing toilets, since that's where household water use quietly adds up. A pressure tank buys you a little water after the power drops, but don't count on much.

If you're on San Lorenzo Valley Water District water, your situation is different from a well household, but storing some water is still smart. Treatment and pumping infrastructure need power too, and storms that cause outages can also cause line breaks. If you have questions about how the district handles outages in your neighborhood, call them at 831-338-2153 and ask. No system can promise uninterrupted service in a storm, so a few stored gallons is cheap insurance either way.

Food, Refrigeration, and Medicine

The rules here are simpler than most people think, and they come straight from foodsafety.gov. An unopened refrigerator keeps food safe for about 4 hours. A full freezer holds a safe temperature for about 48 hours, and a half-full one for about 24. So the strategy is: don't open the doors unless you have to, and eat from the pantry first. Perishables like meat, poultry, fish, eggs, and leftovers that spend more than 2 hours above 40 degrees should be tossed. Frozen food that still has ice crystals, or that reads 40 degrees or below, can be safely refrozen. Never taste something to decide whether it's safe. When in doubt, throw it out.

An appliance thermometer in the fridge and one in the freezer take the guesswork out of all of this, and a cooler with ice extends your window if the outage stretches on.

Refrigerated medication has its own clock. Ready.gov advises discarding refrigerated medicine after a day without power unless the label says otherwise, and calling your doctor or pharmacist right away for a replacement. If your household depends on any medication that's temperature-sensitive, ask your pharmacist ahead of time exactly how long it lasts warm, and write the answer on the box.

One more habit that pays off: when an outage starts, unplug electronics and appliances. Power sometimes comes back with surges that can damage what's plugged in. Leave one lamp on so you know when service returns.

If Someone Depends on Powered Medical Equipment

If anyone in your home relies on electricity for medical needs, get them enrolled in PG&E's Medical Baseline Program now. Eligibility is based on medical need, not income. Enrollees get extra electricity at the lowest rate tier, and, more importantly for us, extra advance notifications before a PSPS, which can include additional calls, texts, and even a knock on the door. A medical practitioner certifies the application, and you can start it online.

Enrollment is not a backup power plan by itself, though. Talk with the medical provider about how long each device runs on battery, what the fallback is, and at what point you'd relocate to somewhere with power. Write that threshold down before you need it.

Check on the Neighbors

This is the part of the plan Boulder Creek already does well. Know which of your neighbors are older, live alone, are on wells, or depend on medical equipment, and trade phone numbers before storm season. A text thread for your road costs nothing and works as long as someone has signal. When an outage hits, a quick knock on the right door can matter more than everything else on this page.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find out when my power will be back on?

Check the PG&E Outage Center map or call the 24-hour outage line at 1-800-743-5002. PG&E posts estimated restoration times once crews assess the damage, and those estimates can change. Nobody else, including this site, can promise a restoration time, so treat PG&E's latest update as the answer.

Will PG&E warn me before a Public Safety Power Shutoff?

PG&E says it tries to notify customers ahead of a PSPS by email, phone, and text, but it also says the first alert may sometimes come the same day power is turned off. Keep your contact info current on your PG&E account, sign up for address alerts if you're not the account holder, and have your supplies ready before fire weather arrives.

How far from the house does a generator need to be?

More than 20 feet from any windows, doors, and vents, per the CDC, and never inside a home or garage, even with everything open. Pair it with a battery-powered carbon monoxide detector near every sleeping area. If you want it wired to the house, have an electrician install a transfer switch so it can't backfeed into the lines.

How long is food safe when the power's out?

About 4 hours in an unopened refrigerator, about 48 hours in a full freezer, and about 24 in a half-full one, according to foodsafety.gov. Toss perishables that spend more than 2 hours above 40 degrees, and never taste food to test it.

What if someone in my house uses an oxygen machine or other powered medical device?

Enroll them in PG&E's Medical Baseline Program for extra notifications before planned shutoffs, and work out a written backup plan with their medical provider: battery runtimes, a charging plan, and the point at which you'd move them somewhere with power.

title: "Utilities, Water, and Waste in Boulder Creek: Who Serves What"
excerpt: Here's the short version for anyone new to town or just tired of guessing. Electricity in Boulder Creek comes from PG&E.
bodyHtml:
Here's the short version for anyone new to town or just tired of guessing. Electricity in Boulder Creek comes from PG&E. Water for most homes comes from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, though plenty of properties are on private wells. Most homes are on septic systems overseen by Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, while a portion of town near the core is on a county-run sewer. Trash, recycling, and organics pickup is GreenWaste Recovery, the county's franchised hauler, and it's not optional anymore. Heating fuel for many households is delivered propane from private suppliers you choose yourself. Internet depends on your exact address, so look it up rather than trusting a list. Because we're unincorporated county, there's no city hall handling any of this; each service has its own provider, and the details below tell you who to call for what.

One standing caveat for the whole page: service territories and providers change, and boundaries up here are parcel-specific. When a decision matters, confirm with the provider directly.

Electricity: PG&E

PG&E is the electric utility for Boulder Creek and the rest of the San Lorenzo Valley. For outages, the PG&E Outage Center has the live outage map, reporting tools, and alert signups, and the 24-hour outage line is 1-800-743-5002. General customer service, including starting or stopping service when you move, is 1-877-660-6789, around the clock.

Two things are worth knowing as a mountain customer. First, PG&E is regulated by the California Public Utilities Commission, which also oversees Public Safety Power Shutoffs, the planned outages PG&E can call during high fire-risk weather. Second, outages are enough of a fact of life here that they deserve their own plan; we cover alerts, generators, and food safety in our power outage plan for mountain households.

On the gas side, don't assume. Some addresses have PG&E natural gas service and many mountain properties don't, relying on delivered propane instead. Check with PG&E about what's actually connected at your address before you buy appliances that expect one or the other.

Water: SLV Water District or Your Own Well

The San Lorenzo Valley Water District has been supplying water in the valley since 1941 and serves Boulder Creek, Brookdale, Ben Lomond, Lompico, Zayante, Scotts Valley, Manana Woods, and Felton through more than 7,900 connections. The district office is right in town at 13060 Highway 9, and the phone number is 831-338-2153. Starting or stopping service, billing questions, and leak reports all go through the district; the website has a start and stop service section, and for anything urgent, call.

Not every Boulder Creek property is a district customer, though. Outlying parcels and some older neighborhoods run on private wells or small shared systems, and the only reliable way to know a specific property's status is to ask the district or look at what the seller discloses in escrow. If you're on a well, you're your own water utility: testing, treatment, and the pump are on you, and Santa Cruz County Environmental Health is the local agency that regulates individual water systems. It's also worth remembering that a well pump needs electricity, which is exactly why well households should read our outage plan article.

Sewer or Septic: Probably Septic

Most Boulder Creek homes treat wastewater on-site with a septic system. The oversight agency is Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, which permits new systems, repairs, and upgrades, and handles maintenance requirements. If you're buying, building, or your drainfield is acting up, they're the call: 831-454-2022, or EnvironmentalHealth@santacruzcountyca.gov. Septic in the San Lorenzo Valley is its own subject, with small lots, steep slopes, and creeks nearby, so involve the county early rather than after a contractor has started digging.

There are two exceptions to the septic rule. A portion of the Boulder Creek community is connected to a sanitary sewer run by the county as County Service Area 7, Boulder Creek Sanitation, which operates its own treatment facility; connected properties pay an annual sewer charge on the property tax bill, and the county's number for the district is 831-454-2160. Separately, the water district owns and operates a small wastewater system serving roughly 56 homes in Bear Creek Estates. If you're not sure which category your property falls into, check your property tax bill for a sewer service charge, or call the county.

Trash, Recycling, and Organics: GreenWaste Recovery

Curbside collection in unincorporated Santa Cruz County, which includes all of Boulder Creek, is franchised to GreenWaste Recovery. Single-family homes get weekly pickup of garbage, recycling, and organics, with garbage carts from 20 to 96 gallons and recycling and organics carts included at no extra charge. To sign up or ask about service on a private road, call 831-426-2711 or email SantaCruzCS@GreenWaste.com.

Here's the part that catches people: service is now mandatory. Under the county's universal service requirement, every developed property in the unincorporated county must either subscribe with GreenWaste or register with the county as a self-hauler, and the county has set a compliance deadline of November 1, 2026, with fines that start at $100 and escalate from there. If you've been quietly trunk-hauling your own bags for years, this is the year to get legal, one way or the other.

For everything the truck won't take, the nearest facility is the Ben Lomond Transfer Station at 9835 Newell Creek Road in Ben Lomond. It also hosts a household hazardous waste.aspx) drop-off on Thursdays from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm, which is where old paint, batteries, pesticides, and motor oil should go; bring proof of residency, and call 831-454-2606 with questions. Check the county Public Works site for the station's current general hours before you load the truck.

Propane and Heating Fuel

If your home heats or cooks with propane, you're dealing with a private, competitive market rather than a utility with an assigned territory. That has a real upside: you can shop. Suppliers differ on per-gallon price, tank rental versus ownership, delivery schedules, and minimums, so it's worth calling two or three, asking neighbors who they use, and requesting current pricing in writing before you commit. If you're new to a propane house, find the tank, learn to read the percentage gauge, and don't let it run low heading into a stretch of winter storms, when delivery schedules get tight for everyone. We're deliberately not naming suppliers here because companies and coverage change; your neighbors' current recommendations will be fresher than any list we could publish.

Internet and Phone

Anyone who promises you a tidy list of Boulder Creek internet providers is guessing, because availability genuinely varies street by street up here. The reliable method takes five minutes: put your exact address into the FCC National Broadband Map, which shows every provider that reports service at your location and what speeds they claim. Treat those listings as claims to verify, not guarantees, then confirm with each provider's own address checker before you schedule anything, and ask the closest neighbors what they actually use and how it holds up in winter. That last step is the one that saves you, because a provider that's fine in town can be a different experience at the end of a long canyon road.

Whatever you choose, remember that home internet and cordless phones die with the power. If you work from home, think through your outage fallback, whether that's a cell plan with hotspot data or simply knowing which nearby spots have power and signal.

Who to Call: The Short List

Keep this somewhere findable. Power outage or downed line area: PG&E, 1-800-743-5002, and stay far away from any downed line. Water service, leaks, or billing on district water: San Lorenzo Valley Water District, 831-338-2153. Septic permits and problems: County Environmental Health, 831-454-2022. Sewer questions in the CSA 7 area: 831-454-2160. Trash, recycling, and organics: GreenWaste Recovery, 831-426-2711. Household hazardous waste: 831-454-2606. And for county emergency alerts, sign up for CruzAware at cruzaware.org so road closures and evacuation notices reach you directly.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if my house is on SLVWD water or a well?

Check your mail for a water bill from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, or call the district at 831-338-2153 with your address and ask. If you're buying a home, the disclosures should say, but verify with the district anyway, because boundaries are parcel-specific and small shared systems exist too.

Is garbage service really required in Boulder Creek?

Yes. The county's universal service requirement says every developed property in the unincorporated county must subscribe with GreenWaste Recovery or register as a self-hauler, with a compliance deadline of November 1, 2026 and escalating fines after that. Call GreenWaste at 831-426-2711 to get set up.

Is Boulder Creek on sewer or septic?

Mostly septic, overseen by County Environmental Health. The exceptions are properties connected to the CSA 7 Boulder Creek Sanitation sewer in part of town, which show a sewer charge on the property tax bill, and about 56 homes in Bear Creek Estates on a small district-run system.

What internet providers serve Boulder Creek?

It depends on your exact address, so look yours up on the FCC National Broadband Map and then confirm with each provider directly. Coverage up here changes street by street, and neighbors on your own road are the best source for how a connection really performs.

Where do I take paint, batteries, and other hazardous waste?

To the household hazardous waste drop-off at the Ben Lomond Transfer Station, 9835 Newell Creek Road, on Thursdays from 7:30 am to 3:30 pm, per the county's HHW page.aspx). Bring proof of residency, and call 831-454-2606 if you're not sure an item is accepted.

title: New to Boulder Creek? The Quick-Start Guide
excerpt: Here's week one in Boulder Creek, in order: sign up for CruzAware emergency alerts, get your PG&E and water accounts sorted, call GreenWaste to set up trash service and learn your pickup day, contact your school's registrar if you've got kids, and grab a library card.
bodyHtml:
Here's week one in Boulder Creek, in order: sign up for CruzAware emergency alerts, get your PG&E and water accounts sorted, call GreenWaste to set up trash service and learn your pickup day, contact your school's registrar if you've got kids, and grab a library card. Boulder Creek is unincorporated Santa Cruz County, so there's no city hall handling any of this. Your services come from a patchwork of county departments and special districts, and this guide walks you through each one. Everything below was verified against official sources on July 15, 2026. Hours and details change, so treat each link as the source of truth.

Sign up for emergency alerts before you unpack

This is mountain country. Fire season, winter storms, and power outages are part of the deal. The county's wildfire preparedness page tells residents to register for CruzAware, the official Santa Cruz County alert system, and that should be the first account you create at your new address. It takes a few minutes online.

While you're at it, set up outage alerts from PG&E. Account holders get notifications about outages at their service address automatically once contact info is current, and you can also follow any address on the outage map without an account. If PG&E calls a Public Safety Power Shutoff during high fire risk, alerts go to account holders without a separate signup.

One more number for your phone: the county's 24-hour road dispatch line, 831-477-3999, listed on the Public Works contact page. That's who you call when a tree or slide blocks a county road. If a line is down or anyone's in danger, it's 911 first.

Put the utilities in your name

Electricity and gas come from PG&E. Start there if the previous owner or tenant hasn't already closed the account.

Water for most homes in town comes from the San Lorenzo Valley Water District, whose office sits right on the main drag at 13060 Highway 9. To start service, fill out the form on the district's Start/Stop Service page at least 24 business hours before you need water in your name. Expect a $20 establishment fee on your first monthly bill. One quirk renters should know: the district keeps accounts in the property owner's name, so if you're leasing, your landlord handles the water account. The office is open weekdays 8:30 a.m. to noon and 1 to 5 p.m., and the main number, 831-338-2153, routes to an answering service for water emergencies after hours.

Not every property is on district water, and plenty of homes up here use septic systems rather than sewer. If you're buying, ask directly whether the house is on district water or a well, and where the septic tank and leach field sit. You'll want that in writing before escrow closes, not after.

Trash, recycling, and organics

Garbage service in unincorporated Santa Cruz County, Boulder Creek included, comes from GreenWaste. Call 831-426-2711 or email SantaCruzCS@GreenWaste.com to set up carts, confirm your collection day, and ask about backyard service if your driveway is the kind of driveway this town is famous for. Collection days vary street by street, so don't assume your day matches your neighbor's across the valley.

For big cleanouts, the county runs its own disposal facilities; the Public Works page lists 831-454-2430 for landfill and recycling questions.

Enrolling kids in school

Boulder Creek sits in the San Lorenzo Valley Unified School District, which covers TK through 12th grade for the whole valley. Enrollment starts with a person, not a portal: the district's enrollment page says to contact your school's registrar first to set up pre-enrollment, after which you'll get online registration instructions to finish within a week.

Have these ready: proof of your child's age, immunization records, an oral health assessment for TK, kindergarten, and first grade, and proof of address, which can be a utility bill, lease, property tax receipt, or similar. For the 2026-27 year, kindergartners need to be five on or before September 1, and TK students need to turn four by that date.

The neighborhood elementary school is Boulder Creek Elementary at 400 Lomond Street, a short walk from downtown, and its site carries current 2026-27 registration information. If you have enrollment questions the registrar can't answer, the district's Student Services office is at 831-336-9678.

Get a library card

The Boulder Creek branch of Santa Cruz Public Libraries is at 13390 West Park Avenue, a block off the highway, and it punches well above its weight after a full remodel. Current hours are Monday through Thursday 10 a.m. to 6 p.m., Friday 11 a.m. to 5 p.m., and Saturday 10 a.m. to 5 p.m., closed Sunday. Free WiFi, computers, kids' programs, and a local history collection make it one of the best rainy-day rooms in the valley.

Getting to Santa Cruz without a car

Santa Cruz METRO runs Route 35, the Highway 9/Scotts Valley line, which connects Boulder Creek to Felton, Scotts Valley, and downtown Santa Cruz. Its Boulder Creek timepoints include Highway 9 at Middleton Avenue in the middle of town and stops out to the Country Club end of Big Basin Way. On weekdays there's also Route 35X, the Santa Cruz/Boulder Creek Express, which skips part of the Scotts Valley routing during commute hours. Schedules shift with service changes, so check the live pages before you build a commute around them.

Fire, sheriff, and who to actually call

Fire protection comes from the Boulder Creek Fire Protection District, with its station at 13230 Central Avenue downtown. The office line, 831-338-7222, is for non-emergency business on weekdays 8 to 5; emergencies always go through 911.

There's no Boulder Creek police department. Law enforcement is the Santa Cruz County Sheriff's Office, which staffs a Boulder Creek Service Center at 13210 Central Avenue with a community deputy; the center's number is 831-454-7440. For things that need a deputy but not lights and sirens, the non-emergency dispatch line is 831-471-1121, per Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1.

Where to get the everyday basics

You can cover most errands without leaving town. Johnnie's Super Market at 13225 Highway 9 has anchored Boulder Creek since 1938 and runs a full grocery with produce, meat, a deli, and a serious wine and spirits section, open daily from 8 a.m. Wild Roots Market at 13159 Central Avenue is the natural-foods counterpart, open daily 9 to 9 with a deli kitchen and local produce. Boulder Creek Pharmacy at 13081 Central Avenue is an independent pharmacy with free delivery throughout the San Lorenzo Valley, open weekdays 9 to 6 and Saturday 10 to 4. And for the endless small fixes an older mountain house demands, Scarborough Home & Garden at 13110 Highway 9 stocks Ace Hardware lines seven days a week.

Your first weekend

Walk the town. The Boulder Creek Recreation and Park District runs Junction Park at Middleton and Railroad Avenues, with a small beach and river access a block from downtown, and Garrahan Park at 15555 Highway 9, with a walking track, soccer field, and play structures, open sunrise to sunset. Dogs are welcome on leash at both. The district's downtown office at 831-338-4144 can fill you in on classes, camps, and community events, which are the fastest way to stop feeling new here.

Frequently Asked Questions

Who provides trash service in Boulder Creek?

GreenWaste handles garbage, recycling, and organics for unincorporated Santa Cruz County, including Boulder Creek. Call 831-426-2711 to set up service and confirm your street's collection day, since days vary by route.

What school district serves Boulder Creek?

San Lorenzo Valley Unified School District, headquartered in Ben Lomond. Start enrollment by contacting the registrar at your child's school; Boulder Creek Elementary is the in-town elementary campus.

Is there a bus from Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz?

Yes. METRO Route 35 runs along Highway 9 through Boulder Creek to Santa Cruz, and the weekday Route 35X express makes the trip faster during commute hours. Check the live schedules on METRO's route pages before relying on a specific trip.

Who do I call when a tree comes down across the road?

For county-maintained roads, the 24-hour road dispatch line is 831-477-3999. If the tree took wires down with it, stay well away and call 911 first. Anything threatening life or property is a 911 call, full stop.

How do I sign up for emergency alerts?

Register at CruzAware, the county's official alert system, and add PG&E outage alerts through your account. Both take a few minutes and matter more here than almost anywhere else you've lived.

title: "How to Set Up CruzAware Alerts (and What They Cover)"
excerpt: If you do one preparedness task this month, make it this: sign up for CruzAware, Santa Cruz County's alert system, at cruzaware.org.
bodyHtml:
If you do one preparedness task this month, make it this: sign up for CruzAware, Santa Cruz County's alert system, at cruzaware.org. It's free, takes about ten minutes, and it's how the county sends evacuation notices and other urgent alerts by text, phone call, and email. In a canyon town like Boulder Creek, where a fire or flood can move faster than word of mouth, that signup is the single highest-value ten minutes of preparedness available. First things first, though: CruzAware is for receiving information. In an actual emergency, call 911. This guide walks through what the system is, exactly what the signup form asks for, and how CruzAware differs from the weather and power alerts you may already get.

What CruzAware Is

CruzAware is the official emergency and non-emergency alert system for Santa Cruz County, operated by the County of Santa Cruz together with Santa Cruz Regional 9-1-1, the agency that dispatches for communities across Santa Cruz and San Benito counties. The county's alert information page describes what it carries: critical information during natural disasters, severe weather events, and public safety incidents, plus non-emergency notices like roadwork and community-level impacts.

The signup site lives at cruzaware.org, which takes you to a registration portal hosted by Genasys, the county's alert platform vendor. The portal describes itself plainly as "the emergency and non-emergency alert and warning system for the residents of Santa Cruz County." It's available in English, Spanish, and Portuguese, and per the county's CruzAware FAQ, your information is used only for alerts and isn't sold or shared outside the system.

Anyone with a stake in the county can register: people who live here, work here, own property here, or just want to keep an eye on conditions for family who do.

What the Alerts Cover

The county's FAQ lists the range: earthquakes, floods, gas leaks, power outages, fires, and other situations that affect safety. Alerts can be geo-targeted, meaning they go to people who registered an address in the affected area. That's the detail that matters most for San Lorenzo Valley residents. An evacuation warning for a specific slice of Boulder Creek reaches the accounts that put an address in that slice, which is why the address step below isn't optional busywork.

You can also opt into topic-based alerts, such as weather or transportation, from your account settings after you register.

There's a limit worth knowing, straight from the county's own FAQ: no system can guarantee delivery. Phones get turned off, texts fail, emails land in spam. The county's advice is to register more than one contact method, and it's good advice.

How to Sign Up, Step by Step

Here's what the actual registration form asks for as of July 15, 2026. You'll need an email address, your phone number, and your street address.

1. Go to cruzaware.org. You'll land on the registration portal. Under the Login box, find "Don't have an account?" and select "Click here to register." 2. Enter your email address. The form marks this required, and it's used for alerts and account recovery. 3. Enter your cell phone number and leave the "Receive text messages on this phone" box checked if the phone can get texts. The form lets you register with phone, email, or both, and recommends both so alerts reach you in more situations. If your phone can't receive texts, an email is required. 4. Enter your full street address and pick the match from the dropdown list as you type. This is required, and it's what ties you to location-specific alerts. The form's own tip: if your address doesn't appear, add your zip code, or click your location on the little map. Rural addresses up here sometimes need that nudge. 5. Create a password, confirm it, and select Register. The form runs a reCAPTCHA check as part of submission.

That's the core signup. If anything on the form looks different when you get there, follow what the portal says; the form is the source of truth and it does get updated.

After You Register: Five Minutes of Fine-Tuning

Once you're in, the account menu lets you strengthen your setup. The county FAQ walks through each item.

Add more locations. You can register multiple addresses: home, work, a kid's school, an aging parent's place in the valley. Each one extends your geo-targeted coverage.

Add more contact methods. Additional emails and phone numbers, including a landline or a family member's cell, can all receive your alerts.

Save the senders in your phone. The portal lists exactly how alerts arrive: phone calls from +1 (831) 453-0048, texts from short code 65513, and email from CruzAware via noreply@genasys.com. Create a contact called "Santa Cruz County Alerts" with that number so a 2 a.m. call gets answered instead of ignored, and add the email address to your safe senders list.

If you have questions the portal doesn't answer, the county's contact for the system is CruzAware@santacruzcountyca.gov, listed right on the portal.

If It Says You're Already Registered

Common situation: you try to sign up and get a pop-up saying your email or number is already registered. That's usually not an error. When the county moved from its old CodeRED system to CruzAware, existing contact information was transferred over. The portal's instruction is to go back to the login screen, choose "Forgot your Password?", and enter your email or cell number to reset access. Once you're in, check that your addresses and contact methods are current, because a migrated account may be carrying old information.

How CruzAware Differs from NWS and PG&E Alerts

These three systems overlap less than people assume, and the safest setup is all three.

CruzAware is county-operated and opt-in. It's the channel for local emergency instructions, including evacuation warnings and orders, and it only knows the contact methods and addresses you give it.

National Weather Service products come from the federal forecast office for the San Francisco Bay Area (based in Monterey), which covers Santa Cruz County. The NWS issues the underlying weather watches and warnings. The most severe ones can also reach your phone automatically through Wireless Emergency Alerts, the federal push system that needs no signup, which the county lists as a separate channel alongside NOAA Weather Radio and the Emergency Alert System. NWS tells you a dangerous storm is coming; CruzAware is where local agencies tell you what to do about it here.

PG&E notifications are separate again. PG&E offers outage updates by text, email, or phone when you sign up through your PG&E online account, and those alerts are about electric service tied to your account. They won't carry evacuation information, and CruzAware won't give you outage restoration estimates. And PG&E's own rule for a downed line applies no matter what alerts you have: call 911 first, then PG&E at 1-800-743-5000.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does CruzAware cost anything?

The county describes it as a free public service, though standard text and call rates from your carrier apply. Registration asks only for contact details, an address, and a password.

Do I need to give my address?

The live form requires an address at signup, and it's central to how the system works: geo-targeted alerts, including weather and evacuation notices for your specific area, depend on it. You can add more addresses after registering.

Will I get alerts if I never sign up?

Some, maybe. The most severe warnings can arrive through Wireless Emergency Alerts, which are automatic. But WEA is a blunt instrument, and county alerts targeted to your neighborhood go only to registered CruzAware contacts. Don't count on being covered without signing up.

Is signing up a guarantee I'll be warned in time?

No, and the county says as much. Delivery can fail if your phone is off, your contact info is stale, or a message lands in spam. Register multiple contact methods, keep them current, and treat alerts as one layer alongside your own awareness of conditions.

How do I update my info or unsubscribe?

Log in at cruzaware.org and use the account menu to edit contacts, addresses, and notification settings. There's an Unsubscribe option that the county FAQ says deletes your contact data from the system.

title: A Responsible Big Basin Day Trip From Boulder Creek
excerpt: Here's the short version: Big Basin Redwoods State Park is open for limited day use from 8 a.m.
bodyHtml:
Here's the short version: Big Basin Redwoods State Park is open for limited day use from 8 a.m. to sunset, the main entrance is about 9 miles up Highway 236 from downtown Boulder Creek, parking reservations are strongly recommended, and all campgrounds in the main park remain closed. The park is still recovering from the 2020 CZU fire, so what's open changes regularly. Before you drive up, check the official Big Basin Redwoods State Park page. That page, not this one, is the source of truth for current conditions, and nothing below is a guarantee of access.

With that said, a Big Basin day trip from Boulder Creek is very doable, and doing it thoughtfully matters more now than it ever did. Here's how to plan it.

Know what you're visiting

Big Basin became California's first state park in 1902, and Boulder Creek has been its gateway town ever since. In August 2020, the CZU Lightning Complex fire changed everything. According to CAL FIRE's incident record, the fire burned 86,509 acres across Santa Cruz and San Mateo counties. It reached Big Basin two days after igniting and, per Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks, burned roughly 18,000 acres inside the park, over 97 percent of it, and destroyed nearly every structure, including the historic Park Headquarters.

The recovery is real, though. State Parks reports that most of the old-growth redwoods survived, new growth is coming in fast, and wildlife has returned. The park reopened for limited access in July 2022, and Sempervirens Fund notes that access has kept evolving since, shaped by both the fire and the severe storms of 2023. You won't find the shaded, built-out Big Basin of old photos. You'll find something rarer: an old-growth forest rebuilding itself in the open sun, with new trail mileage opening in phases.

Set your expectations accordingly. Facilities are limited, shade is thinner than it used to be, and a trail that was open last month may be closed today.

Reserve your parking before you go

Day-use parking reservations are highly recommended. As of this writing, State Parks lists regular-sized autos at $6 plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 per vehicle without a reservation, and parking is limited enough that showing up without one is a gamble on busy days. Book through the Big Basin parking reservation page run by Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks. Passenger vehicles use the main form; disabled parking and bus or horse trailer parking have separate reservations.

Two more parking notes. Oversized vehicles and trailers can't be accommodated for day use right now, so leave the RV at camp. And fees or rules can change, so confirm current pricing on the official park page when you book.

Getting there from Boulder Creek

From downtown Boulder Creek, turn onto Highway 236 (the Big Basin Highway) and follow it about 9 miles to the main entrance. Every road into Big Basin is curvy, and lower 236 is narrow, so give yourself 25 to 30 unhurried minutes, watch for cyclists, and use the turnouts if you're holding up locals.

If you'd rather not drive in, there are two alternatives worth checking. On summer weekends between Memorial Day weekend and Labor Day weekend, State Parks has run a free pilot shuttle from the Saddle Mountain temporary parking area along Highway 236 to the main day-use area. And Santa Cruz METRO's Route 35B offers weekend bus service to the park. Both are subject to schedules and seasons, so verify each is running before you count on it.

What's open in the park

Conditions change, so treat this as a snapshot from mid-July 2026, not a promise.

The classic first stop is the Redwood Loop Trail, an easy 0.6-mile walk through the old-growth grove near the historic park core. It's flat, family-friendly, and the best place to see fire scars and new growth side by side on trees that have been alive for well over a thousand years.

The trail network keeps growing. Friends of Santa Cruz State Parks reports that a recent round of work opened about 4 more miles of trail, restoring access to Sempervirens Falls, along with segments of the Skyline to the Sea Trail from the main day-use area to China Grade Road and beyond toward Castle Rock, plus the Hihn Hammond connector along Opal Creek. At the same time, closures still happen: State Parks currently lists Hollow Tree Trail as temporarily closed from winter storm damage. Check the current trail map on the park page before you commit to a route.

Camping is the big limitation. All campgrounds in the main park are closed, and no camping reservations are available there. The one exception on the property is Rancho del Oso, the coastal side of Big Basin near Waddell Beach, which is partially reopened and offers walk-in and bike-in camping. If an overnight matters to you, plan around that or look at other parks nearby.

Expect limited services inside the park: temporary facilities rather than the old lodge-and-store setup. Bring your own water, food, and layers.

How to visit responsibly

A recovering forest asks a little more of its visitors. Stay on open trails, both for your own safety and because the regrowth on either side of the tread is doing important work. Respect every closure sign; they usually mark hazard trees, unstable ground, or active restoration, even when the path beyond looks fine. Pack out everything you bring in. Skip drones and speakers. And don't count on cell coverage in the basin, so download maps ahead of time and tell someone your plan.

Weather deserves respect here too. Heat, wind after storms, and winter road damage all affect this park, and this site never interprets conditions for you. Check the forecast, check the park page the morning you go, and if the park says don't come, don't come.

One more way to be responsible: support the recovery. Sempervirens Fund, the land trust that helped create the park in the first place, publishes updates and supports the ongoing restoration work.

Make Boulder Creek part of the day

The most direct route into Big Basin runs straight through downtown Boulder Creek, and the town is the natural place to bookend the trip. Fuel up, grab coffee and sandwiches, and buy anything you forgot before you head up 236, because you won't find services along the way. On the way back down, dinner or a cold drink in town lands differently when you've spent the day in the basin.

That's not just a nicety. Gateway towns carry the quiet costs of a beloved park, and they felt the fire as directly as the park did. Spending your money in Boulder Creek is the simplest way a day visitor helps this corner of the Santa Cruz Mountains keep rebuilding. If you want the fuller story of the fire and what it meant here, read our companion piece on the events that shaped Boulder Creek once it's live.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a reservation to visit Big Basin?

For parking, a reservation is highly recommended rather than strictly required as of this writing: $6 per regular vehicle plus a $2 reservation fee, or $10 without one, per State Parks. Spaces are limited, so booking ahead through the official reservation page is the safe move. Confirm current rules on the park page before you go.

Is camping open at Big Basin?
